items.headline: Mali accuses military officers of working with jihadis to carry out attacks against government
items.body: Mali's military prosecutor says officers were complicit in recent coordinated insurgent attacks. This signals deep fractures within the junta, weakening its ability to fight rebels.

items.headline: Mexican governor indicted by the US for drug trafficking says he'll step down
items.body: The governor of Mexico's Sinaloa state is temporarily resigning after a US indictment. The move strips his immunity, escalating political fallout from alleged cartel ties.

items.headline: Tanzania shuts camp housing thousands of Burundi refugees
items.body: Tanzania closed the Nduta refugee camp, repatriating thousands of Burundians amid coercion claims. This forces a vulnerable population into an uncertain and potentially unsafe future.

items.headline: Spirit Airlines ceases operations, stranding passengers across United States
items.body: Spirit Airlines announced Saturday it would immediately wind down all operations after a federal bailout failed to materialize. The eighth-largest US carrier had 9,000 scheduled flights through May, leaving thousands stranded.

items.headline: Iran submits negotiation proposal to United States through Pakistan mediators
items.body: Iran sent a proposal to end the war to US mediators from Pakistan, state media reported. President Trump confirmed he is reviewing the Iranian proposal.

items.headline: Cambodia repatriates 635 Thai nationals from scam operations
items.body: Cambodia returned 635 Thai citizens allegedly working in online fraud from a single building Thursday. Thai authorities are screening returnees for prosecution in cross-border fraud networks.

items.headline: Chinese court rules AI job replacement unlawful in landmark case
items.body: A Hangzhou appeals court ruled that dismissing a tech worker after replacing his job with AI was unlawful. Legal scholars call it a reassuring signal for labor rights as China pushes widespread AI adoption.
